Enjoy Kakuro the way it was meant to be—clear, simple, and satisfying.
Kakuro is a classic logic puzzle that blends the structure of a crossword with the number-based reasoning of Sudoku. This easy-level collection is designed for a smooth, frustration-free solving experience, making it perfect for beginners and anyone looking to relax with clean, approachable puzzles.
Inside You’ll Find:
• 100+ Easy Kakuro Puzzles• One Puzzle Per Page for maximum clarity• Large, Easy-to-Read Grids• Clean, Distraction-Free Layout• Solutions included at the back
Each puzzle is carefully crafted to be engaging without being overwhelming, helping you build confidence as you solve. Whether you’re new to Kakuro or simply prefer a more relaxed pace, this book offers a comfortable and enjoyable way to sharpen your mind.
Designed for a perfect balance of readability and portability, with one puzzle per page in a comfortable, easy-to-handle format.
Perfect for:
• Beginners learning Kakuro• Casual puzzle solvers• Adults and seniors seeking easy-to-read layouts• Relaxing at home or on the go

It's my favorite of the kakuro books I've tried.

productPageReviews.reviewTitle: I've lost my husband to Kakuro.
productPageReviews.reviewDescription: My husband is too busy with his new Green Belt Kakuro, so I'll write the review from my perspective.  This is his second Kakuro book and more challenging than the easy first one.  Since he has his nose in the book more than ever, I'll say it's a winner. It started out easy and they get a little harder as it goes along.  He's entertained and kept busy so now he doesn't notice how much time I spend in Facebook.  I better order the next level book soon!!
productPageReviews.reviewedIn: Reviewed in the United States on October 13, 2012

productPageReviews.reviewTitle: Great supplement or alternative for Sudoku-ers
productPageReviews.reviewDescription: I came across Kakuro by happenstance.  I was in OfficeMax waiting in line when I spotted a beginners Kakuro book.  I never heard of Kakuro before but the book looked intriguing so I thumbed through it and liked the concept.  I'm not a good Sudoku person and this looked like a nice alternative because I do like puzzles.  Well, I smashed through that book and I was hooked.That lead me to step up to the Green Belt Kakuro which I think was a perfect graduation for me.  The Green Belt Kakuros are bigger and offer a few more combinations which tax my brain just enough to be challenged but not enough to quit.  I'm starting to pick up on certain combinations and the Green Belt is beginning to get a little easier.  Whereas it took me 45+ minutes to do a single puzzle before, midway through the book I'm down to about 1/2 hour for a puzzle.  That's not to say that I'm no longer challenged.  There are some puzzles I've had to retrace and see where did I add wrong or where did I assume a correct combination because now I'm in a impossible situation.  I think that's all part of the fun and the challenge and it makes me slow down and be more attentive the next time.
productPageReviews.reviewedIn: Reviewed in the United States on February 13, 2014

productPageReviews.reviewDescription: I love Kakuro puzzles, and this series is among my favorites.  That said, it is among my favorites because it has a real variety of puzzles.  Every book in this series has a bunch of puzzles that are on the easier side, a bunch that are of medium difficulty, and a bunch that are more difficult.  They could have done a better job sorting the puzzles and putting them in the most appropriate level book.  I actually have found more easy puzzles in the green belt book than in the white belt book.  I have not yet finished the Black Belt book (I completed the White belt, the Green belt, and the Brown belt), but that one does seem to be a bit more difficult so far.  Otherwise, all three of other books are about the same level (or variety of levels).

productPageReviews.reviewTitle: This is by far the best Kakuro Series on the market (at least that i ...
productPageReviews.reviewDescription: This is by far the best Kakuro Series on the market (at least that i have been able to find).I got introduced to kakuro through one of those generic easy > medium > hard books and instantly liked it.  I bought the mensa kakuro book, which turned out to be a great book.  Then came the black belt and 2nd degree black belts, also great kakuro books.  And now the Absolutely Nasty Kakuro series.  I bought level 4 first.  Lots of math calculations in order to solve, but not impossible!  After i completed level 4 i read someones review that level 3 is challenging as well, so bought it and they were right.  It is different than level 4 but by no means easy.  Because of a lack of other options on the market i just bought level 4 again.  What makes the Mensa, Black Belt, and Absolutely Nasty books so great?  one puzzle per page, large print, and the pages are big enough to add notes to them in order to solve the puzzles.If you love Kakuro, try out these books!

productPageReviews.reviewDescription: My favorite kakuro books are the Second-degree Black Belt and the Second-degree Brown Belt books which up to now were the best and the hardest Kakuro books.  The present book, Absolutely Nasty Kakuro Level Four, is harder than these:  I solve better than 9 out of 10 of the (2nd-degree) Brown and Black Belt books, but so far have only been able to solve 2 out of the first 6 puzzles I've tried from this new book.The quality of paper used for this new series is even better than that used for the Brown and Black belt books.  Also, the puzzles are even larger (by 1 additional square across, and 2 additional squares down).  The spiral binding is also nice.  So, altogether, these new books set a new standard of excellence for Kakuro books!

productPageReviews.reviewDescription: I absolutely love this series.  It is a good size -- easy for travel or just use at home.  I use mine as a travel journal of sorts. productPageReviews.reviewDescription: I love that the grids are 13x22 & only have 6 "warm-up" puzzles. They're easy enough to solve without needing hints, but challenging enough to make you think. I ordered the book over a year ago & liked it so much, this is actually my 2nd copy! This is the best series of Kakuro books I've found. productPageReviews.reviewTitle: Great book format but less difficult than I expected
productPageReviews.reviewDescription: This is the first time I've tried a Kakuro book in the Martial Arts series so I chose Brown Belt level (3rd of 4 difficulty levels), which the author labels as "hard".  These puzzles are easier than I was expecting, though still fun to do. If you're familiar with the "Absolutely Nasty" Kakuro series I'd say these Brown Belt puzzles are similar to Absolutely Nasty level 2 (out of 4). I'm going to try out the Black Belt book, which the authors rate as "super tough".The format of these Martial Arts Kakuro books is nice. One 13-wide by 21-high puzzle per page, on 5.5" by 8.5" pages. The squares are plenty big enough to be able to write several numbers in when writing possible values, and the paper is good quality for erasing without wrinkling or tearing through. So my only quibble is with the difficulty ratings-- I think the Brown Belt ("hard") puzzles are easier than one might expect for that term. The book format is 5 stars, then I took 1 star back for their estimated rating for difficulty.

productPageReviews.reviewTitle: I really like the spiral binding
productPageReviews.reviewDescription: I just finished an old Kakuro book that I bought several years ago, and suddenly found again.  The levels went from basic to "diabolical".  As the puzzle difficulty increased, the squares got smaller and smaller, making it really difficult to write in the number possibilities in tiny, almost microscopic print in each square.  That book was also done on "comic book" paper, which was a huge bleed-through problem.  If I used sharp, thin-lead pencils, erasures were a mess.I bought the first three levels of this series based on the reviews here, and just received them yesterday.  I am extremely surprised at how well they are done.Kakuro, by the very nature of this logic puzzle genre, needs to have enough space in each square to write in the possible answers for that square, and erase or blot out the eliminated possibilities - hopefully, in not quite microscopic text.This series, definitely not made for beginners, has the paper quality that a Kakuro fan needs.  The paper is bright, and slick and strong enough for erasures, and prevents bleed-through.  The squares are a consistent, adequate size throughout the books.I really like the spiral binding, which allows for easy writing on each page, whether on the right or left side, with no worry of splitting the binding.With only one completely finished Kakuro puzzle book in my experience, I've found that the three that I just bought in this series are a huge jump above that old "comic book quality" type that I recently finished.  That one had a few puzzles with non-exclusive puzzle solution areas.  There were two alternate solutions that both worked.  I hope I don't encounter that with these puzzles.Picking off "low hanging fruit" I'm half way through two of the puzzles in the first book - the first and the last one.  I don't see any progressive difficulty difference from the first puzzle to the last one.  I can see that these puzzles will provide a lot of "throne room" entertainment for a long time to come.For those that find Sudoku just too easy, these Kakuro books are just what is needed, especially for us senior citizens with enough "throne room" time available to complete puzzles in a day or two.

aPlusContent.title: From the Publisher
aPlusContent.rawText:
From the Publisher
Strengthen your mental muscles as you try to solve puzzles like these: 
Calcudoku 
A Calcudoku puzzle uses the numbers 1 through 4 for a 4-by-4 puzzle, 1 through 5 for a 5-by-5 puzzle, and so on. Like in sudoku, each row and column must contain each number exactly once (creating what mathematicians call a Latin square). But, unlike a sudoku puzzle, none of the numbers are provided. You need to use logic to place the numbers in each cell according to the clues given in each “cage.” 
Sudoku 
The name “sudoku” is an acronym, coined in the 1980s for a longer Japanese phrase that translates to “the digits must be single.” The first sudoku-type puzzles appeared more than 100 years ago in a French newspaper, but sudoku as we know it didn’t show up in American puzzle books until the late 1970s. 
The rules are simple: Fill in the numbers 1 through 9 within the grid so that each number appears exactly once in each row, column, and 3-by-3 subsquare. 
Masyu 
Masyu (pronounced “MA-shoo”) is a Japanese word meaning “evil influence.” The objective is to draw a loop (consisting of horizontal and vertical lines) that passes through each box that contains a circle. When the line passes through a black circle, it must turn 90 degrees while passing straight through both adjacent squares. The rules for white circles are the opposite. The line must pass straight through white circles but must turn 90 degrees through one or both adjacent squares.

items.title: Calcudoku
items.text: A Calcudoku puzzle uses the numbers 1 through 4 for a 4-by-4 puzzle, 1 through 5 for a 5-by-5 puzzle, and so on. Like in sudoku, each row and column must contain each number exactly once (creating what mathematicians call a Latin square). But, unlike a sudoku puzzle, none of the numbers are provided. You need to use logic to place the numbers in each cell according to the clues given in each “cage.”

items.title: Sudoku
items.text:
The name “sudoku” is an acronym, coined in the 1980s for a longer Japanese phrase that translates to “the digits must be single.” The first sudoku-type puzzles appeared more than 100 years ago in a French newspaper, but sudoku as we know it didn’t show up in American puzzle books until the late 1970s. 
The rules are simple: Fill in the numbers 1 through 9 within the grid so that each number appears exactly once in each row, column, and 3-by-3 subsquare.

productPageReviews.reviewDescription: Love this book so much! I’ve always loved logic puzzles and was looking for a book with a good variety. Difficulty progresses throughout the book, so I often “warm up” with a puzzle earlier in the book before switching to something more challenging. productPageReviews.reviewTitle: Unique take but a few errors
productPageReviews.reviewDescription: There are five main puzzle types throughout the book with an occasional unique twist where you find a word in the solution that culminates into a large puzzle at the end of the book. I loved this feature and got really into flipping to find these special puzzles so I could solve the big one at the end. I’ve never seen anything like this and it kept me very engaged and interested.Just personal preference and no impact on my rating, but it’s worth noting - I have apps for sudoku and nonograms so it was just tedious to do these by hand. Just keep that in mind if you’re in the same boat. Logic grid puzzles are the only reason I buy puzzle books and I was disappointed to find these felt pretty repetitive (the same issue I have with logic puzzle apps).As others have said, there are quite a few errors in the solutions and hints. The author seems like a huge puzzle nerd so I’m not sure how so many mistakes got through him and editor/publishers.The explanation on the puzzle type I hadn’t seen before (masyu) was confusing until I looked at the solution a few times to try and figure out what he was trying to say. A lot of puzzle books assume you already know how to play everything so I guess I can’t complain too much.The handful of completely new puzzles in the back were hit and miss for me. There are no instructions because the author expects you to use logic (!) to figure it out. Sometimes I got it, sometimes I didn’t even understand after looking at the solution. And some of them make you question how any one would figure it out (e.g., figuring out a pun from a blocky picture). Maybe I’m just dumb.. but I don’t think a few lines of instruction would go amiss.Finally, the binding is terrible for this type of book. I appreciate the quality paper and quality look of the cover, but a looser spine or spiral bind would make this much more enjoyable to use.

productPageReviews.reviewTitle: Nice kakuros... a few things to note
productPageReviews.reviewDescription: These kakuros are different than ones I had done before.  The biggest change was how big they are.  They have I think over 200 boxes to fill in, and most of the clues involve a large number of numbers (8-9).  I've done 6, and so far I find them challenging, but solveable.  The biggest issue I find is that they really take a while... I'm pretty decent at these puzzles, but it still takes me a couple hours to solve them, and one simple wrong calculation (out of hundreds) ruins the puzzle.  I find myself checking the answers frequently to make sure I'm on track (no cheating, just confirming).The spiral bound is very helpful. I wish there was a tad more room around the edges for notes/calculations, but it's overall well designed.  Due to the complexity of the puzzles, I've found it is difficult to work on for 10-15 minutes at a time and expect to make a ton of progress... much easier if you stay engrossed.If you are still reading this...here's a kakuro ethical question I've wondered about.  Suppose for a corner you find that there are exactly 3 solutions that work with the information you currently have.  You determine that no matter what other progress you make, 2 of the solutions (a+b) will either always both work or always not work.  Since there is only one working solution to each puzzle, you know it's actually the third one (c), but your only basis for outlawing a+b is because you know there is always one solution.  Is that fair logic to use?

productPageReviewsFromOtherCountries.reviewTitle: Enjoyed doing these very large puzzles
productPageReviewsFromOtherCountries.reviewDescription: I just finished this book.According to the introduction to the book, the first 12 puzzles are hard, the next 30 are very hard, and the final 30 are very very hard.The first 12 puzzles don't contain any 9-digit sums. That doesn't mean they're easy, though. Although I didn't have any great difficulty solving them, I found them challenging enough to be enjoyable, even though I'm an experienced Kakuro puzzler.The 30 puzzles in the "very hard" group do include 9-digit sums. As with the puzzles in the first group, I didn't have any great difficulty solving these, but I found them challenging enough to be enjoyable and interesting.When I got to the last 30 puzzles, the increase in difficulty was immediately apparent. I usually found that a puzzle would seem fairly easy at first, and I could get through the majority of it without any problem, but then I'd reach a point where I'd realize that the last part wasn't going to be easy at all. Before I got to the end I often had to resort to trial and error to solve the final portion of the puzzle. Some of the harder puzzles in this book seemed equally as difficult as some of the harder puzzles in Level Four of the Absolutely Nasty Kakuro series. Some of these took me more than a day to solve.All of the puzzles in this book are on very large (20 x 27) grids, so even the not-so-hard ones take quite a while to complete. You really need to keep your focus when working on them so you don't get near the end of such a large puzzle only to discover that you've made a mistake somewhere and need to start all over again. That happened to me a few times even though I consider myself to be pretty careful.Although the puzzles in this book vary somewhat in their level of difficulty, I enjoyed doing all of them. I'll probably buy this book's companion volume, 72 Titanically Tricky Puzzles, before long.
The best of Kakuro books

productPageReviews.reviewDescription: If you like Kakuro or anything similar, you will really enjoy this book! It is packed full of great puzzles.  I've purchased this book once a year for the past few years, and I plan on continuing to do just that. I've been doing Kakuro for over 20 years and this is my favorite book.I find the puzzles are challenging enough so that I don't get bored, but not so hard that I just can't get anywhere. This is one in a series of Kakuro puzzle books with their difficulty levels being rated like a Karate Belt color. I have tried several of the other levels. In fact, I started with a Black Belt because others have said I'm much better than them. I don't see myself that way at all, but I also hate paying good money for a book of puzzles that are so easy that I'm bored to death.  Suffice it to say that there was NO way I was ready for a Black Belt!So I scaled down until I did the First Degree White Belt - or the easiest in the series. That was fun, but I took it up just a notch to this one, which seems to be my sweet spot -- but for good reason. I think the puzzles in this book are nicely challenging. I am able to finish all of them, and most without any problem. They are just challenging enough that they are still great fun!The biggest reason this book works well for me is due to the amount of space provided to write in. There isn't much space to write number combinations in those teeny tiny squares. To solve more difficult puzzles, most people need more room to jot down the various possibilities.  I can still write several possible combinations in the squares, where it is really needed. But once you start noting all of the combinations, square after square, it all gets very messy. That's when I start making mistakes. I write very legibly -- even teeny tiny writing.So with this book, I force myself to do some of the combinations in my head and then keep them as I look at the alternatives surrounding that area. I still write some teeny tiny number combinations in the corners as needed, but it's not as bad as having to deal with squishing way too much into every small square. That's where the difficulty level for this book is just right. There is still enough challenge, but not so hard that I'm making myself crazy between trying to squish in everything needed and then trying to read it!So if you consider yourself an average Kakuro puzzle solver, I think you'll enjoy this book a great deal!

productPageReviews.reviewTitle: The puzzles are harder than expected but...
productPageReviews.reviewDescription: I know that the puzzles will seem like they've stumped you at times in this book, as the other reviewers stated.  I was getting frustrated with it too until I started doing what I usually don't do, (and I don't mean cheating), I began to look at sums and realize that there would have to be a certain number in it, and there was only one such place that number could go.  What I'm saying is that you'll have to switch the traditional way you've solved these puzzles - but that's what makes this so great.  I appreciate the high level of challenge.

products.bookDescription: Sum-thing big is on the horizon Kakuro puzzles (also known as cross sums) are like a combination of crosswords and sudoku: each clue in the grid tells you the sum of the digits in its answer, and just like sudoku, digits are never repeated within an answer. From this simple premise, extremely challenging puzzles can be constructed, and that’s just what Conceptis Puzzles has done for these collections, which each contain 72 supersized kakuro, offering massive solving pleasure for the brave souls who love tackling such challenges.

products.bookDescription:
"Kakuro Puzzle Book for Adults" is the ultimate collection of puzzles for those who want to test their numerical skills and challenge their problem-solving abilities. This book is filled with a variety of Kakuro puzzles of different difficulty levels, making it perfect for both beginners and seasoned players.
Kakuro, also known as Cross Sums, is a numerical puzzle game that requires you to fill a grid with digits that add up to specific values. With each puzzle, you'll be able to enhance your math and logic skills, as well as your focus and concentration.
This Book is designed to keep you engaged and entertained for hours, while also providing you with an opportunity to improve your cognitive abilities. The puzzles in the book range from easy to expert, so you can start with the simpler ones and work your way up to the more difficult ones.
Whether you're looking for a fun way to pass the time or want to improve your problem-solving skills, "Kakuro Puzzle Book for Adults" is the perfect choice. So, pick up a copy today and start solving puzzles!

productPageReviews.reviewTitle: A good book in a good series
productPageReviews.reviewDescription: This is the fourth in a series of Kakuro books, and the hardest. The puzzles are indeed tough, and I often had to look up the answers to figure out what I had done wrong, or to get a cell number to keep me going, but they were solvable for the most part.I also purchased the Green Belt (not-so-easy) and Brown Belt (hard) books. Conceptis Puzzles did a good job of sorting the puzzles by level of difficulty. I started with the Black Belt, as it was the first one available on Amazon. I recommend you start with an easier edition and work your way up. The puzzles are the same size in all the books, 12 columns by 21 rows, which is a good value.My only complaint is that the gimme's (two number combinations with only one answer) are repetitive. For example, for a six figure 38, the 3 almost always comes from a 5, while the 5 comes from a 6. Otherwise, there is a good amount of variety among the puzzles.
